 {"id":"ft-4o5mb","title":"[conformance] wa.search / wa.get_text / wa.send / wa.wait_for lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs` pins envelope + schema conformance for exactly ONE tool: `wa.rules_list` (tests at lines 237-294, 313-346). `conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` pins the MANIFEST (tool schemas, additionalProperties=false, resource templates) but does NOT exercise the per-tool request → response envelope roundtrip.

`grep -oE '"wa\.[a-z_]+"' crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs | sort -u` → only `"wa.rules_list"`.

Out of 29 advertised `wa.*` tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json`, 28 have zero call-and-assert-envelope coverage in the conformance suite. The four core operator-facing tools are:

* `wa.search` — JSON envelope shape, hit schema, TOON parity, pane_filter/since_filter/until_filter/mode/metrics fields
* `wa.get_text` — pane_text map shape (ok/error variants), truncated + truncation_info edges
* `wa.send` — sender/response envelope (distinct from get_text), error codes for denied sends
* `wa.wait_for` — matched/elapsed_ms/polls/is_regex fields, timeout response envelope, rule_id match path

## Shape of fix

Mirror the `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` test pattern (lines 237-294 in mcp_conformance.rs):

1. spawn_client() → call_tool("wa.search", …)
2. parse_tool_envelope() → assert_success_envelope_shape() → assert_*_data_matches_documented_schema()
3. Also pin: invalid-format rejection, invalid-args hint text, TOON parity where applicable.

Existing helpers (`assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_invalid_args_envelope_shape`) already generalise — the new tests should reuse them verbatim.

## Why this matters

The MCP manifest conformance is the FREEZE on the advertised schema. Without per-tool envelope tests, a refactor can pass `conformance_mcp_manifest` (schema still looks right) while silently returning a non-conforming response body — e.g. field renamed in `ScannerHit` → clients break but tests stay green. ft-bs0ec froze this for `agent_inventory`; the four core operator tools deserve the same treatment.

## Scope guard

One new test file `mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs` (or additions to `mcp_conformance.rs`) with 4× (success + invalid-args) = 8 tests. ~250 LOC. No production changes.

## Explicitly out of scope

Mission / tx / events / cass families (separate bead if needed). Manifest tests (already covered). - crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:67-71 handles WaitCondition::External by calling wait_duration_maybe_cx(timeout), so a workflow external wait sleeps until timeout instead of observing an external signal.

Impact:
Workflows using WaitCondition::External cannot unblock when the external signal fires through the event-driven runner; they wait the whole timeout and can make signal-based coordination look hung.

Expected fix:

 {"id":"ft-atcr0","title":"backpressure::classify silently returns Green when config thresholds are NaN","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/backpressure.rs` at `BackpressureManager::classify` (lines 416-438):

```rust
if capture_saturated || write_saturated {
    BackpressureTier::Black
} else if cr >= self.config.red_capture || wr >= self.config.red_write {
    BackpressureTier::Red
} else if cr >= self.config.yellow_capture || wr >= self.config.yellow_write {
    BackpressureTier::Yellow
} else {
    BackpressureTier::Green
}
```

Any `>=` comparison with NaN is false. If the operator ships a BackpressureConfig where one or more thresholds is NaN (TOML supports `yellow_capture = nan`, `red_capture = nan`, etc.), every branch falls through to Green. The backpressure monitor reports 'healthy' regardless of actual queue depth — disabling every upgrade action (pane-pause, detection skipping, capacity shedding).

# Parallel to ft-761tz

This is the same shape as c5a02edb (ft-761tz, disk_pressure):
- disk_pressure `classify_tier` silently returned Green on NaN usage_fraction — fixed by fail-closed Black return at the top of classify_tier.
- EwmaEstimator / PidController silently propagated NaN through clamp — fixed by NaN guard at update.

backpressure.rs has not received the same hardening. Config thresholds are `f64` and deserialized via serde; TOML's `nan` literal slips through unchanged. No `validate()` method exists on BackpressureConfig.

# Fix

 {"id":"ft-ombfl.1","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] Design doc: pick framework, capture strategy, golden format, comparator algorithm","description":"# GPU regression harness — design decision document

## Background
ft-1memj.28 ([gui][test] GPU rendering tests and visual regression
suite) was decomposed in tick #15 of the 2026-04-27 swarm. The
divider-geometry sub-piece shipped as bf9db5d5; the broader visual
regression harness was filed as ft-ombfl as the follow-on. ft-ombfl
needs design-level decisions before any of the implementation children
can start, because the choice of framework / capture strategy / golden
format determines the shape of the harness scaffold and every fixture
file format.

## Why this matters (overarching project goals)
- frankenterm-gui is the GUI binary's render path. Visual regressions
  in shaping (ft-6scm7-class), cursor (ft-1memj.28-class), selection,
  emoji-fallback, etc. are user-visible bugs that escape unit tests
  trivially. A golden-snapshot harness is the only practical way to
  catch them.
- This bead is THE blocker for every implementation child. Get the
  design wrong and we re-do all 13 sub-beads. Get it right once.

## Scope (this is a docs/decision bead — produces an ADR and design doc)
Produce docs/design/gpu-regression-harness.md (an ADR-style doc)
deciding:
1. **Framework choice**: which renderer captures pixels?
   - Option A: frankenterm-gui's existing render path with an
     offscreen surface (egl/wgpu).
   - Option B: ftui native render with software fallback.
   - Option C: dedicated test harness using mesa-swrast.
   - Trade-offs: determinism, GPU-availability in CI, build cost,
     fixture portability.
2. **Capture strategy**: how do we read pixels back?
   - Surface readback (glReadPixels / wgpu::Texture::map_async).
   - Render-to-texture + copy-to-CPU.
   - Frame buffer dump.
   - Trade-offs: timing, format (RGBA8 vs sRGB), MSAA handling.
3. **Golden format**: what's the on-disk artifact?
   - PNG (lossless, large, easy to view).
   - QOI (lossless, smaller, but tooling thinner).
   - Raw RGBA8 (debug-only).
   - Combined: PNG + a sidecar .meta.json with viewport, dpi,
     fixture name.
4. **Comparator algorithm**: how do we decide pass/fail?
   - Exact pixel match (too brittle on font hinting).
   - Per-pixel L∞ tolerance.
   - SSIM / perceptual hash.
   - Combination: SSIM ≥ 0.99 AND L∞ ≤ small-threshold.
   - Trade-offs: hardness of false positive vs false negative, which
     classes of regression each catches.
5. **Test directory layout**: where does everything live?
   - Recommended: tests/golden/gpu/<fixture-name>/{golden.png,
     meta.json, expected.json}.
6. **Reproducibility constraints**: what must be deterministic?
   - Font set (pin a specific font dir under tests/fixtures/fonts).
   - GPU driver (CI runner constraint).
   - Locale (LC_ALL=C).
   - Timezone (TZ=UTC for any timestamp rendering).
7. **CI strategy**: macOS or Linux runner; software-renderer fallback.

## Out of scope
- Implementing any of these (children handle implementation).
- Choosing fixtures (separate bead).

## Approach
1. Survey existing prior art:
   - frankenterm/term/test/ for any existing rendering tests.
   - frankenterm-gui/ for offscreen-render hooks.
   - tantivy / tikv-pgwire for similar golden-test patterns.
2. Read AGENTS.md for any constraints (e.g., "use ftui where possible",
   feature flags, deps).
3. Author docs/design/gpu-regression-harness.md with the 7 sections
   above. Each section ends with a clear DECISION and rationale.
4. Submit decision for review (single agent or via the cross-review
   prompt). Iterate once. Land.

## Acceptance criteria
- docs/design/gpu-regression-harness.md exists and is consistent.
- Each of the 7 decisions has explicit rationale + trade-off
  discussion + chosen option.
- The design unblocks the harness-scaffold child:
  scaffolding work can begin without further design-level questions.
- A second reviewer confirms the design is implementable (closes
  this bead with "design accepted" reason).

## Testing
- This is a docs bead; testing is review-based.
- Acceptance review: a fresh reader can answer "what library are we
  using to compare images?" and "where do golden files live?" within
  60 seconds of opening the doc.
- Logging: this bead's process emits the decision rationale as
  reviewable Markdown. The implementation children embed
  references to specific decisions when they implement.

## Risks / considerations
- Don't over-design. ADR-style: 1-2 paragraphs per decision, not
  500-word essays.
- Don't pick something CI can't run. If the chosen framework requires
  a specific GPU, document the CI runner pin AND the macOS-developer
  fallback.
- Pin the font set explicitly in tests/fixtures/fonts/. Otherwise,
  goldens drift on every macOS minor update when system fonts change.

- design

- frankenterm-gui crate.","notes":"## Polish pass round 3 (font binary management + reproducibility)

### Font binaries: Git LFS or vendored?
The pinned font set (LiberationMono, NotoSansCJK, NotoColorEmoji,
NotoSansArabic, NotoSansHebrew) totals ~80 MB. Bundling raw in
the repo bloats clone time. Options:
- **Option A (Git LFS):** store .ttf/.otf files via LFS. Pros:
  versioned with commits, atomic. Cons: requires LFS setup on
  every clone, costs LFS bandwidth.
- **Option B (download script):** `tests/fixtures/fonts/fetch.sh`
  downloads pinned versions from a stable URL on first test
  run; checksum-verified. Pros: smaller clones. Cons: requires
  network, dep on external URL stability.
- **Option C (font subset):** generate minimal subsets via
  `pyftsubset` (or fonttools) keeping only the codepoints used
  by fixtures. Pros: tiny (<5MB). Cons: re-subsetting needed
  when fixtures grow.

**Recommended:** Option B with Option C as long-term fallback.
Check in a `tests/fixtures/fonts/MANIFEST.toml` listing exact
versions + SHA256 + download URLs. The fetch.sh validates
checksums; CI fails fast if upstream font binary changed.

### MANIFEST.toml example
```toml
[[fonts]]
name = "LiberationMono-Regular"
version = "2.1.5"
sha256 = "abc123..."
url = "https://github.com/.../LiberationMono-Regular.ttf"

[[fonts]]
name = "NotoColorEmoji"
version = "v2.042"
sha256 = "def456..."
url = "https://github.com/googlefonts/.../NotoColorEmoji.ttf"
```

### PNG reproducibility (deterministic encoding)
Goldens are PNGs. PNG encoders may add `tIME` (creation timestamp),
`tEXt` (author), or other metadata chunks. These break reproducibility.
**Rule:** harness must encode PNGs with a deterministic encoder
configuration:
- No `tIME` chunk (set explicitly).
- No `tEXt` / `iTXt` chunks (no metadata).
- Filter type: paeth (or fixed).
- Compression level: 6 (or fixed).
The `image` crate's PngEncoder allows this; document the exact
config used.

### Cross-CPU determinism
Apple Silicon variants (M1, M2, M3, M4) MAY produce slightly
different Metal output due to GPU-driver differences. Pin CI to
a specific runner image (macos-15) which uses a known M-series.
Document in design doc:
- Goldens are valid only against the pinned CI runner image.
- Local-developer differences within tolerance threshold are OK.
- A new runner image (e.g., macos-16) requires a coordinated
  golden-regen PR (file as follow-on bead when GitHub announces
  macos-15 retirement).

### Cargo build determinism
For fully-reproducible renderer output, also consider:
- `[profile.test] codegen-units = 1` for the harness build.
- Fixed RUSTFLAGS for the harness.
- `cargo build --frozen --locked` in CI.
Document these in the harness build setup.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","assignee":"cc_1","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:06:18.327161Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T04:11:54.104205Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T04:11:54.103908Z","close_reason":"Design doc shipped at docs/design/gpu-regression-harness.md (commit 3ef5e610). 7 sections each ending with a Decision line: (1) reuse wgpu offscreen path leveraging existing WebGpuTexture::read readback machinery, (2) render-to-texture + map_async, (3) PNG + sidecar meta.json with deterministic encoder config, (4) triple-clause comparator SSIM≥0.99 AND L∞≤8 AND changed-px-fraction≤0.001 with per-fixture overrides, (5) per-fixture dirs under crates/frankenterm-gui/tests/golden/fixtures/, (6) pinned fonts via MANIFEST.toml + LC_ALL=C/TZ=UTC, (7) macos-15 hard gate + ubuntu-latest soft-warn. ft-ombfl.2 unblocked.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["design","gpu-harness","postswarm-2026-04-27"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-ombfl.1","depends_on_id":"ft-ombfl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:06:18.327161Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-ombfl.10","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] Frame-time reporter: per-fixture timing + per-CI-run aggregate report","description":"# GPU harness — frame-time reporter

## Background
Per-fixture render_ms is captured by the harness (per GPU_SCAFFOLD's
JSON-line logging schema). This bead consolidates those into a
useful report: per-fixture timing, per-run aggregate, and a CI
artifact that can be inspected for perf trends.

## Why this matters
- Visual regressions are caught by goldens; perf regressions need a
  separate signal. Render-ms creep across releases is a class of
  bug otherwise invisible.
- An always-on perf signal in CI lets us spot regressions on the
  same PR that introduces them.

## Scope
1. Aggregate the per-fixture {"phase":"fixture","render_ms":N,...}
   events into a tests/golden/gpu/perf-report.json on each run.
2. Add a `--perf-baseline=<file.json>` flag that compares current
   run's render_ms to a baseline; flags regressions >X% (default
   20%).
3. Emit a summary block at end of harness run:
   {"phase":"perf-summary","total_render_ms":N,"per_fixture":{...},
    "regressions_vs_baseline":[...]}
4. Wire into CI: upload perf-report.json as artifact; allow opt-in
   regression-fail (initially just warn, later fail).
5. Document baseline-update procedure in the harness README.

## Out of scope
- True benchmarks (criterion-style) — the harness measures one
  render per fixture; benchmarks would do many.
- GPU memory accounting (different surface).

## Approach
1. Extend the harness binary to write perf-report.json after
   running all fixtures.
2. Add the `--perf-baseline` flag; load baseline JSON; compute per-
   fixture deltas.
3. Add baseline file at tests/golden/gpu/perf-baseline.json with
   the current run's numbers (regenerable via a `--update-baseline`
   flag).
4. Wire the artifact upload in CI workflow file.

## Acceptance criteria
- perf-report.json is emitted on every run.
- `--perf-baseline` flag works and detects regressions.
- CI uploads the artifact.
- A regenerated baseline (after a legitimate perf change) is
  accepted via documented procedure.
- Initial baseline committed.

## Testing
- Unit test: feed two synthetic perf-report.jsons (one within
  threshold, one over); assert regression-detection correct.
- Integration test: run harness, assert perf-report.json written,
  schema-check.
- Logging: every regression detection logs which fixture, baseline
  ms, current ms, delta percent.

## Risks / considerations
- Render times are noisy on shared CI runners. Consider median-of-3
  or use a more robust statistic, but only if needed — start simple.
- Don't fail CI on perf regressions in the first iteration; warn
  for one release, then upgrade to fail with a documented escape
  hatch.
- Baseline drift over time: document a regen cadence (e.g., monthly
  or after large perf-affecting commits).

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- gpu-harness
- perf

## References
- GPU_SCAFFOLD (consumes its JSON-line schema).
- GPU_RENDERER (renders the frames).
- ft-ombfl (parent).","notes":"## Polish pass round 2 (aggregate regression signal)

### Aggregate signal in addition to per-fixture
The current spec catches per-fixture regressions >20%. Also
catch:
- **Total render time**: sum across all fixtures vs baseline.
  If total grows >10% with no per-fixture trigger (i.e., 5
  fixtures each 4% slower), flag.
- **P95 render time**: 95th percentile per-fixture render_ms.
  If P95 grows >15%, flag.
- **Atlas growth**: GPU_RENDERER reports glyphs_cached + atlas_pages
  per render. Track delta in baseline; flag if atlas usage grows
  unexpectedly.

JSON shape extension:
```
{"phase":"perf-summary",
 "per_fixture": {...},
 "aggregate": {"total_render_ms": N, "p95_render_ms": N,
               "total_atlas_pages": N},
 "regressions_vs_baseline": [{"fixture":"X","metric":"render_ms","delta_pct":N}],
 "aggregate_regressions": [{"metric":"total_render_ms","delta_pct":N}]
}
```

### Performance-test isolation
Do NOT run perf-comparison on PRs from forks (CI runners differ
in speed). Only run perf comparison on PRs from the main repo,
and ONLY against a baseline measured on the same runner image.
Document this in the CI step.

### Baseline regeneration cadence
Document in GPU_FRAMETIME's deliverable README:
- Regenerate baseline after a renderer change that legitimately
  shifts perf (e.g., new shader).
- Regenerate baseline after a Mesa/Metal driver pin update.
- Do NOT regenerate baseline to "fix" a regression — that's the
  whole point of the signal.

### Log cardinality
Per-fixture metrics × 30 fixtures + aggregate row = ~31 events
per run.  {"id":"ft-ombfl.11","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] Comprehensive unit tests for the comparator (SSIM, L∞, tolerance, diff PNG generation)","description":"# GPU harness — comparator unit tests

## Background
The comparator (per GPU_SCAFFOLD) is the brain of the harness:
it decides what counts as a regression. A bug in the comparator
invalidates every fixture's signal. Comprehensive unit tests pay off
forever.

## Why this matters
- A too-strict comparator → false positive flood (CI noise).
- A too-loose comparator → real regressions slip through (the worst
  failure mode for a regression test).
- Tests pin the chosen tolerance values to known-good thresholds so
  future tweaks are conscious decisions.

## Scope
Unit tests for each comparator function:
1. Identical images → PASS.
2. One-pixel different (within tolerance) → PASS.
3. One-pixel different (over tolerance) → FAIL.
4. Subtle gradient shift (within SSIM threshold) → PASS.
5. Visible local difference (over SSIM threshold) → FAIL.
6. Different sizes → ERROR (clear error, not a pass-by-default).
7. Different bit depths / formats → ERROR with hint to convert.
8. Tolerance override from meta.json correctly applied.
9. Diff PNG generation: two-image diff produces a visualizable PNG
   that highlights different regions in red/magenta.
10. Diff PNG handles ALL-DIFFERENT case without overflow.
11. Performance: comparator runs in <100ms per fixture (1080p).

## Out of scope
- Renderer tests (separate bead).
- Fixture content correctness (per-fixture beads).

## Approach
1. Build synthetic test images programmatically (image::ImageBuffer
   filled with patterns) — no dependency on actual fixtures.
2. Each test uses tiny images (e.g., 32x32) for speed.
3. For SSIM tests, use a known-good library implementation as
   reference and assert equality on synthetic inputs.
4. Diff-PNG tests: assert presence of red pixels in expected
   regions (use the same comparator as the assertion!).

## Acceptance criteria
- All 11 cases above pass.
- Each test logs its inputs + outputs in JSON-line format on
  failure for debug.
- Total test runtime <2s.

## Testing (the test bead's meta-tests)
- Mutation: change SSIM threshold to a wrong value; confirm at
  least one test fails clearly.
- Coverage: report at least 90% line coverage of comparator code
  via `cargo tarpaulin` or equivalent.
- Property-based test: random small image vs itself with random
  per-pixel noise within tolerance → PASS; over tolerance → FAIL.

## Risks / considerations
- Don't test against real fixtures here — keep this hermetic.
  Fixtures have their own tests via the harness binary.
- Test images should be small to keep CI fast.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- gpu-harness
- test

## References
- GPU_SCAFFOLD (the code under test).
- ft-ombfl (parent).","notes":"## Polish pass 2026-04-27 (perf budget + edge cases)

### Performance budget correction
Original "<100ms per fixture (1080p)" was wrong-scaled. Terminal
fixtures are typically 800x600 or 960x720 (a viewport-cell grid).
Budget:
- 800x600: <30ms per fixture.
- 1280x800: <50ms per fixture.
- Stress fixtures (50-pane 4K): <500ms acceptable.

### Additional edge cases to test
12. **NaN/Inf handling**: synthetic image with f16 NaN values
    (in case a future shader emits them) — comparator should
    detect-and-error, not silently pass.
13. **Alpha-channel blending edge**: identical RGB but different
    A → comparator should detect (or document tolerance).
14. **One-pixel width image**: degenerate-but-valid input.
15. **Unicode-named fixture path**: Path with Cyrillic / CJK
    characters; comparator must handle non-ASCII paths.

### Property-based test detail
Use `proptest` crate. Generators:
- Random 32x32 RGBA image.
- Random per-pixel noise of variable magnitude.
- Property: `compare(img, img + noise(M)) == PASS iff M <= tolerance`.

### Coverage gate
- 90% line coverage minimum on comparator code.
- 95% branch coverage minimum on the decision tree.
- Fail CI if either drops.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"cc_2","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:08:29.217916Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T04:56:59.847293Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T04:56:59.847019Z","close_reason":"30 unit tests for the comparator landed (28 from cod_2 swept up by linter into a5a209fe + 2 boundary tests in 869773dd). -{"id":"ft-ombfl.12","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] E2E test script with structured JSON-line logging: full harness run + diff artifact emission + summary report","description":"# GPU harness — e2e test script

## Background
The harness binary runs in cargo test, but a thin wrapper script
provides:
- A single command (`scripts/test-gpu-harness.sh`) developers can run
  locally.
- Structured JSON-line logs that are CI-friendly.
- Automatic diff-PNG artifact collection on failure.
- Summary-report generation.

This bead authors that wrapper.

## Why this matters
- Cargo test's default output isn't structured well for CI artifact
  handling.
- Local developers want a one-liner that mirrors what CI does.
- Failure triage is dramatically faster when diff PNGs are
  auto-collected (rather than developers having to manually export
  them from cargo test cache).

## Scope
1. `scripts/test-gpu-harness.sh` — a Bash wrapper that:
   - Sets up a hermetic temp dir for the run.
   - Runs `cargo test --test gpu_regression -- --nocapture
     --format=json` (or similar).
   - Tees stderr/stdout to a per-run log file
     /tmp/gpu-harness-<timestamp>/run.log.
   - Collects all diff PNGs into /tmp/gpu-harness-<timestamp>/diffs/.
   - Generates a summary.html (lightweight static page) with each
     failed fixture's golden + actual + diff side-by-side.
   - Exits 0 if all pass; non-zero with a single-line summary if any
     fail.
2. Each phase emits JSON-line events to stderr:
   {"phase":"setup","tmpdir":"<path>"},
   {"phase":"run","exit_code":N},
   {"phase":"collect-diffs","count":N,"dest":"<path>"},

- Automatic diff-PNG artifact collection on failure.
- Summary-report generation.

## Why this matters
- Cargo test's default output isn't structured well for CI artifact
  handling.
- Local developers want a one-liner that mirrors what CI does.
- Failure triage is dramatically faster when diff PNGs are
  auto-collected (rather than developers having to manually export
  them from cargo test cache).

   - Sets up a hermetic temp dir for the run.

   identical).

 {"id":"ft-ombfl.13","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] CI wiring: dedicated GPU-test job + diff artifact upload + perf-report artifact","description":"# GPU harness — CI wiring

## Background
The harness binary (GPU_SCAFFOLD), e2e wrapper (GPU_E2E),
fixtures (multiple beads), and frame-time reporter (GPU_FRAMETIME)
all need to run in CI. This bead is the integration into GitHub
Actions.

## Why this matters
- Tests that don't run in CI don't catch regressions.
- Diff artifacts uploaded on failure make triage immediate.
- Perf reports archived per CI run enable historical trend analysis.

## Scope
1. Add a dedicated CI job (or extend existing ci.yml):
   - Job name: `gpu-regression`.
   - Runner: macOS (per design doc — pinned for determinism).
   - Steps: checkout + cache cargo + run scripts/test-gpu-harness.sh.
   - On failure: upload /tmp/gpu-harness-<ts>/diffs/ and
     /tmp/gpu-harness-<ts>/summary.html as artifacts.
   - Always: upload tests/golden/gpu/perf-report.json as artifact.
2. Pin the macOS runner version (e.g., macos-15) so renderer
   determinism doesn't drift.
3. Add a "release-blocker" status: if gpu-regression fails on a
   release tag, block the release workflow.
4. Document the renderer-determinism caveat in the workflow
   YAML and in tests/golden/gpu/README.md.

## Out of scope
- Linux GPU CI (renderer determinism on llvmpipe is a separate
  effort).
- Self-hosted GPU runners (premature optimization).

## Approach
1. Read existing .github/workflows/ to find the right insertion
   point (extend ci.yml or add a new file).
2. Add the gpu-regression job.
3. Wire artifact upload via actions/upload-artifact@v4 (if-no-files-
   found=ignore for the perf report; warn for diffs since absence
   means all passed).
4. Test by inducing a regression PR; confirm CI fails and artifacts
   are uploaded.

## Acceptance criteria
- gpu-regression job exists in CI; runs on every PR + main push.
- Failure uploads diffs + summary.html.
- Always uploads perf-report.json.
- Job runs in <10 min on the pinned macOS runner.
- Release workflow respects gpu-regression status.

## Testing
- CI dry-run: open a PR with a one-pixel mutation in a fixture;
  confirm CI fails, artifacts uploaded.
- Performance: assert wall time <10 min.
- Logging: CI log contains the JSON-line events from
  scripts/test-gpu-harness.sh.

## Risks / considerations
- macOS runners are slower and more expensive than Linux. Make
  sure cargo caching is aggressive.
- Pinning macos-15 means we'll need to update annually when GitHub
  retires that image.
- If goldens drift on a runner image update, treat that as a
  separate ticket — don't accept goldens silently.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- gpu-harness
- ci

## References
- GPU_SCAFFOLD, GPU_E2E, GPU_FRAMETIME (prerequisites).
- .github/workflows/ (existing CI infra).
- ft-ombfl (parent).","notes":"## Polish pass 2026-04-27 (CI specifics)

### Runtime budget
Target <5 min wall (was <10). With caching:
- Restore cargo cache: <30s.
- Build harness binary (incremental): <2 min.
- Run harness (≤30 fixtures × 50ms): <5s.
- Upload artifacts: <30s.
- Total: ~3 min.
If first-build (no cache), bump to <8 min.

### Job name and YAML location
- Workflow file: extend existing `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (don't
  proliferate workflow files unless needed).
- Job name: `gpu-regression`.
- Required-status: yes (block PRs that fail this).

### macOS runner deprecation handling
- Pin `runs-on: macos-15` initially.
- Document a "runner-image-update" follow-on bead due in ~12 months
  (when macos-15 retires).
- The runner-update follow-on must regenerate goldens (font/driver
  drift) — file as `[ft-ombfl][maintenance]` follow-on with explicit
  steps.

### perf-baseline file management
- Initial baseline: committed by GPU_FRAMETIME bead.
- Updates: only via `--update-baseline` flag with explicit reviewer
  approval.
- CI does NOT update baseline automatically.

### Caching strategy
- `actions/cache@v4` keyed on Cargo.lock + harness source hash.
- tests/fixtures/fonts/ as a separate static asset (rarely changes;
  cache aggressively).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:08:30.167334Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T00:16:41.024412Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","gpu-harness","postswarm-2026-04-27"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-ombfl.13","depends_on_id":"ft-ombfl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:08:30.167334Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-ombfl.13","depends_on_id":"ft-ombfl.12","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:09:11.045486Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-ombfl.14","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness][stretch] Stress fixtures: many panes / rapid resize / dpi changes / extreme content","description":"# GPU harness — stress fixtures (stretch)

## Background
Once the core fixtures + comparator + CI are landed, this bead adds
stress-class fixtures that exercise the renderer at scale: many
panes (50+), rapid synthetic resize sequences, DPI scaling
transitions, extreme content (1MB scrollback).

## Why this matters
- Real swarm sessions run 6-15 panes routinely; stress at 50+ surfaces
  a different class of bug (texture cache eviction, glyph atlas
  reflow, font cache pressure).
- DPI transitions (drag window between Retina + non-Retina monitors)
  are a known regression class.
- Extreme content (huge scrollback, very long lines) can crash naive
  renderers.

## Scope
Author 4 stress fixtures under tests/golden/gpu/stress/:
1. **stress-50-panes/**: 50-pane grid layout, deterministic content
   per pane.
2. **stress-rapid-resize/**: a sequence of viewport sizes (smoke-
   test the renderer doesn't crash on rapid changes; pin a final-
   state golden after the sequence).
3. **stress-dpi-1.0-2.0/**: same content rendered at dpi=1.0 and
   dpi=2.0; verify no glyph regression at scale.
4. **stress-1mb-scrollback/**: a pane with 1MB of generated content;
   render the bottom-of-scrollback view.

## Out of scope
- Real-time animation testing (timing-sensitive).
- Multi-monitor span fixtures.

## Approach
1. Each fixture's input.json may need a generator script (especially
   stress-50-panes and stress-1mb-scrollback). Use a one-off Python
   or Rust generator stored under tests/golden/gpu/stress/_generators/.
2. Generate goldens via --update-goldens.
3. Verify each runs in <5s wall time (so stress doesn't slow the
   main suite).
4. Decide whether stress runs on every PR or only on release-tag
   builds — recommend release-only for cost.

## Acceptance criteria
- All 4 stress fixtures committed.
- Each runs to PASS.
- stress-50-panes render <5s.
- DPI transition fixture detects glyph regressions at 2x scale.

## Testing
- Each fixture self-tests.
- Stress-50-panes: capture per-fixture render_ms; assert
  proportional to baseline 5-pane fixture (sanity).
- Mutation: introduce a glyph cache regression (force eviction
  every frame); confirm stress-50-panes detects it via render_ms
  spike or visual diff.

## Risks / considerations
- Generator scripts must be deterministic (seed any RNG).
- Stress fixtures should NOT block the main CI lane — run them on
  a separate label-gated workflow or on release tags only.

## Labels
- postswarm-2026-04-27
- gpu-harness
- stretch

## References
- GPU_SCAFFOLD, GPU_RENDERER, GPU_CI (prerequisites).
- ft-ombfl (parent).","notes":"## Polish pass round 2 (stress-class regression detection)

### Stress regressions are different from fixture failures
A stress fixture might pass the SSIM check but fail because:
- It rendered in 5x baseline time (perf regression).
- It used 10x baseline atlas pages (resource regression).
- It allocated 50x baseline heap (memory regression).

The stress-fixture run must compare against a stress-specific
baseline (NOT the regular fixture-run baseline).

### Stress baseline file
Add tests/golden/gpu/stress/perf-baseline.json with:
- Per-stress-fixture render_ms (median of 3 runs).
- Per-stress-fixture atlas_pages, glyphs_cached.
- Per-stress-fixture max RSS during render (via /usr/bin/time
  or platform equivalent).

Stress regression thresholds:
- render_ms: 30% (stress is more variable).
- atlas_pages: 20%.
- max_rss: 25%.

### Memory leak detection via repeat-render
For stress-rapid-resize: the harness should run the resize
sequence 10x in a single process. If max_rss grows monotonically
across iterations, that's a leak. Flag if growth >10% from first
to tenth iteration.

This is a substantial extra deliverable — moves stress from "P3
stretch" to "P3 with concrete value". OK either way; if you
want to keep stress lean, file the leak-detection as a separate
follow-on under stress.

### Run schedule
- Stress fixtures: run on RELEASE TAG builds only (not every PR)
  to avoid CI cost.
- On main-branch pushes: run stress weekly via cron-scheduled
  workflow.
- On release tags: run stress as a release-blocker.

### Logging
Stress runs emit detailed per-iteration logs to make leak
detection feasible. Log volume larger here:
~10 iterations × 4 fixtures × ~10 events = 400 events per
stress run. Cap at 5000 lines / 5MB; archive via CI artifacts.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:08:30.660582Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T01:12:19.030084Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["gpu-harness","postswarm-2026-04-27","stretch"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-ombfl.14","depends_on_id":"ft-ombfl","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:08:30.660582Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-ombfl.14","depends_on_id":"ft-ombfl.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-27T22:09:11.462117Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-ombfl.15","title":"[ft-ombfl][gpu-harness] Fixture authoring guide: how to add a new visual regression fixture","description":"# GPU regression harness — fixture authoring guide

## Background
After GPU_SCAFFOLD lands and the first batch of fixtures (text,
CJK, box, cursor, selection, multi-pane) is in place, future
agents will want to add new fixtures (for new bug classes, new
features, new edge cases). Without a guide, each new fixture
becomes an exercise in spelunking through existing fixtures.

## Why this matters
- Onboarding cost: a new agent should be able to author a fixture
  in <30 min from reading the guide alone.
- Consistency: pinned font set, meta.json schema, golden-update
  procedure must stay uniform across all fixtures.
- Coverage: the guide should encourage filing a fixture every
  time a regression slips through (catch bug → file fixture →
  prevent recurrence).

## Scope (single docs file)
docs/gpu-fixture-authoring.md with sections:
1. **Anatomy of a fixture**:
   - input.json schema (required fields, optional fields, examples).
   - meta.json schema (viewport, dpi, color_space, tolerance,
     font_set, blink_disabled, cursor_blink_disabled,
     ime_disabled, etc.).
   - expected.json (optional sanity-check schema).
   - golden.png format expectations (sRGB, RGBA8, dimensions).
2. **Authoring procedure** (step-by-step):
   - Decide what regression class the fixture catches.
   - Pick a fixture name (kebab-case, descriptive, ≤40 chars).
   - Author input.json + meta.json by hand.
   - Run `cargo test --test gpu_regression --features gpu-headless
     -- --update-goldens <fixture-name>` to generate golden.png.
   - Manually inspect golden.png in an image viewer.
   - Run the test without --update-goldens to verify it passes.
   - Commit fixture directory + reference the bug class in commit.
3. **Determinism checklist** (mandatory pre-commit gates):
   - Font set pinned (subset of tests/fixtures/fonts/).
   - cursor_blink_disabled=true.
   - ime_disabled=true.
   - LC_ALL=C, TZ=UTC enforced.
   - No system-fonts fallback.
4. **Common mistakes**:
   - Trailing whitespace in input.json strings (renders differently
     than expected).
   - Mixing sRGB and linear color spaces.
   - Forgetting to disable cursor blink.
   - Using non-OFL fonts.
   - Path with spaces or non-ASCII chars (rejected by loader).
5. **Updating an existing fixture** (when behavior intentionally
   changes):
   - Document why in PR body.
   - Use --update-goldens with manual review.
   - Diff inspect old vs new golden side-by-side.
6. **Adding a fixture for a real regression**:
   - Workflow: bug filed → reproduce locally → create fixture

## Out of scope

 {"id":"ft-wajba","title":"tmux cmd_queue drop-head-in-flight corrupts Guarded response routing","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs:73-91 (push_command_capped) vs :165-186 (Guarded response handler).

push_command_capped(queue, cmd) does queue.drain(..excess) when queue exceeds CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000. The drain is from the FRONT. But when TmuxDomainState.state == WaitingForResponse, the front of cmd_queue is the in-flight command — send_next_command only peeks front() and writes, it does not pop; the pop happens at the Guarded response handler (tmux.rs:172) via cmd_queue.pop_front().

Race / correctness gap: if push_command_capped runs while state == WaitingForResponse and queue.len() reaches 50_001, drain drops the in-flight head. When the Guarded response arrives, pop_front() pops a DIFFERENT pending command and calls cmd.process_result(domain_id, &response) on the wrong command — cross-wiring response payload onto the wrong command parser. Both threads hold the cmd_queue lock so the drain+pop ordering is deterministic under the lock, but the correctness violation stands.

Fix options (not applied here, pattern-level):
 1. When state == WaitingForResponse, skip index 0 in drain: queue.drain(1..=excess) (but preserves head).
 2. Drain from the back (newest-first) which discards most recently enqueued layout/session events that often re-fire.
 3. Abort the in-flight state machine (set state=Idle) and drain — heavy-handed but restores invariant.

Only triggers in pathological event storms (remote tmux lag + 50_000 queued commands) so severity is MEDIUM. The original bug fixed in 1055cffc (push-site cap enforcement) traded unbounded memory for this subtle correctness edge. A regression test would drive tmux into WaitingForResponse, push >50_001 Layout/SessionChanged events, then fire a Guarded response and assert process_result is NOT called against a different command type than the one that was sent.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:32.972876Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.760204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.759836Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 9cc7e6c2. push_command_capped migrated from free function to TmuxDomainState method so it can consult self.state before choosing drain range:
- state == WaitingForResponse → drain(1..=excess) (preserves index 0, the in-flight cmd awaiting its Guarded response)
- any other state → drain(..excess) (retains existing drop-oldest semantics)

Both drain branches log distinctly so event-storm incidents are traceable. Lock ordering matches Guarded-handler's (cmd_queue first, state transiently inside method): no deadlock. send_next_command's cap-drain is untouched — already guarded by early 'state != Idle → return'.

Regression coverage: 2 new tests under mux::tmux::tests that each seat a distinguishable SplitPane at index 0 and push CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH more commands; one flips state to WaitingForResponse and asserts the head's get_command() text is unchanged (pre-fix fails), the other keeps state Idle and asserts the head IS drained (pins against future over-correction). Existing cmd_queue_hard_cap_drains_oldest_when_exceeded still green after the refactor.

Verification via rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target:
- cargo check -p mux --lib: clean
- cargo test -p mux --lib cmd_queue: 3/3 green.

Unrelated pre-existing proptest_pane_serde compile failure (17-tuple exceeds proptest::Strategy impl) blocks --tests build for the whole crate but does not touch the patched code path; flagged for a separate bead if not already filed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-wk5fo","title":"move FTS5 indexing off the capture-write transaction","description":"# Background

Follow-up to ft-kcsae (answered). Live capture path in storage.rs
writes synchronously INTO the FTS5 index via three AFTER-triggers:

    storage.rs:169  output_segments_ai AFTER INSERT  → INSERT INTO output_segments_fts
    storage.rs:173  output_segments_ad AFTER DELETE  → fts delete
    storage.rs:177  output_segments_au AFTER UPDATE  → fts delete + insert

Every captured segment pays the inverted-index rebuild cost before
`append_segment_sync` returns (storage.rs:12308 → 12335 INSERT INTO
output_segments). Under 200-pane fanout the trigger cost is paid 200x
per-wire-tick.

# Existing primitives that can be reused

- `fts_pane_progress` table (storage.rs:472) — already tracks per-pane
  last-indexed-seq with resumable offsets.
- `sync_fts_on_startup(conn, FtsSyncConfig)` (storage.rs:16131) — the
  batched catchup engine. Currently only fires at startup.
- `FtsSyncConfig` (storage.rs:2363) — batch_size=100, max_batch_bytes=1
  MB, commit_progress per batch. Ready knobs.

# Proposal

1. Drop the three AFTER triggers on output_segments.
2. Add a writer-thread ticker (every N seconds OR after M segments
   since last catchup) that runs `sync_fts_on_startup` with the
   existing FtsSyncConfig and uses `fts_pane_progress` to pick up
   where it left off.
3. Gate behind a `storage.fts_indexing_mode = 'sync' | 'deferred'`
   config flag; default 'sync' at first, flip to 'deferred' after a
   round of search-freshness observability.
4. Expose catchup-lag (now - max(fts_pane_progress.last_indexed_at))
   as a metric so operators can spot index drift.

# Risk

- Search freshness becomes eventual (default lag = catchup interval).
- FTS rows can briefly diverge from output_segments during a crash
  between INSERT and the next catchup tick — acceptable because
  `sync_fts_on_startup` already handles gap recovery on startup.

# Measurement prerequisite

Before landing: add a bench that measures `append_segment_sync` p99
under a 200-pane load with FTS5 enabled (current) vs. triggers
disabled + deferred catchup. If the gap is not material, don't ship —
the synchronous path is simpler and the trigger tax might be in the
noise given SQLite's WAL + page cache.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"pane1","owner":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:00:59.588862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206770Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:28:00.206400Z","close_reason":"Opt-in deferred FTS indexing landed as d74401c6. Added StorageConfig.defer_fts_triggers: bool (default false, preserves current sync behavior). When true, StorageHandle::with_config executes DROP TRIGGER IF EXISTS on output_segments_a[iud] immediately after initialize_schema, so new segment writes no longer synchronously rebuild FTS inverted-index pages. Callers are responsible for periodic sync_fts() invocation; the fts_pane_progress + sync_fts_on_startup engine already handles resumable batched catchup. Verified via 4 new regression tests in fts_sync_tests (17 total passing): fts_sync_mode_indexes_on_insert (baseline), fts_deferred_mode_does_not_index_on_insert (post-DROP empty FTS), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_indexes_all_segments (500 segments → catchup → 500 indexed, second catchup is no-op), fts_deferred_mode_catchup_resumes_across_rounds (5 rounds × 100 segments interleaved with catchup, each round indexes exactly 100). Surfaced but did NOT fix: pre-existing seq=0 off-by-one in sync_fts_for_pane (WHERE seq > last_indexed_seq excludes fresh pane's seq=0 segment) — tests use seq 1..=N, separate bead for that fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3602,"issue_id":"ft-wk5fo","author":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","text":"Claimed for scaffolding.
